coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted carbon dot grafted YVO4:Eu3+ for the ratiometric fluorescent determination of paranitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wei
Zhang, Haoran
Chen, Shi
Liu, Yingliang
Zhuang, Jianle
Lei, Bingfu
coredata.dc:description: A facilely prepared ratiometric fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor has been constructed for highly sensitive and selective detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) using carbon dots (CDs) as the target sensitive fluorophore and YVO4: Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) as the reference fluorophore. Through the hydrolysis and condensation reactions of the silica precursor, CDs and YVO4 Eu3+ NPs can be incorporated into silica networks through silylation reaction by one pot synthesis procedure. The as-prepared fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor shows characteristic fluorescence emissions of CDs (blue) and YVO4:Eu3+ (red) under a single excitation wavelength. With the addition of 4-NP, the fluorescence of CDs is selectively quenched, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence response. Under optimum conditions, the proposed sensor exhibits a high sensitivity with a linear range from 0 to 12.0μM and shows the limit of detection as low as 0.15μM in the determination of 4-NP, which is probably benefits from the tailor-made imprinted cavities for binding 4-NP. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of 4-NP in real water samples and human urine samples with great potentials for monitoring of 4-NP in environmental application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
YVO4:Eu3+
4-Nitrophenol
Molecularly imprinted
Sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the YVO4:Eu3+ NPs colloid
Synthesis of CDs@MIPs, C-Y@MIPs and C-Y@NIPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province
Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51203053 , 21371062 and 51372091 ), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the one pot preparation of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs sensor. (b) Schematic illustration for the molecular imprinting mechanism of CDs in the silica.
(a) TEM image of CDs. (b) TEM image of YVO4:Eu3+. (c) TEM image of C-Y@MIPs.
TEM images of C-Y@MIPs and corresponding elemental mapping images of Si, C, N, Y, V, O and Eu, respectively.

(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs, YVO4: Eu3+ and C-Y@MIPs. (b) XRD patterns of CDs, YVO4: Eu3+ and C-Y@MIPs. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (excited at 360nm), YVO4: Eu3+ (excited at 275nm), and C-Y@MIPs (excited at 330nm). (d) Photostability of C-Y@MIPs upon the addition of 6.0μM 4-NP within 60min (excited at 330nm).
Fluorescence spectra of (a) pure CDs aqueous solution, (b) CDs@MIPs, (c) C-Y@NIPs, and (d) C-Y@MIPs upon the exposure to different concentrations of 4-NP under 330nm excitation, respectively.

(a) Stern-Volmer plots from C-Y@MIPs, C-Y@NIPs, and pure CDs aqueous solution upon the addition of 4-NP from 0 to 12.0μM. (b) Quenching efficiencies of C-Y@MIPs and C-Y@NIPs upon the addition of different kinds of 12.0μM phenols (4-NP, 2-NP, phenol, hydroquinone).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of molecularly imprinted carbon dot grafted YVO4:Eu3+ for the ratiometric fluorescent determination of paranitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facilely prepared ratiometric fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor has been constructed for highly sensitive and selective detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) using carbon dots (CDs) as the target sensitive fluorophore and YVO4: Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) as the reference fluorophore. Through the hydrolysis and condensation reactions of the silica precursor, CDs and YVO4 Eu3+ NPs can be incorporated into silica networks through silylation reaction by one pot synthesis procedure. The as-prepared fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor shows characteristic fluorescence emissions of CDs (blue) and YVO4:Eu3+ (red) under a single excitation wavelength. With the addition of 4-NP, the fluorescence of CDs is selectively quenched, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence response. Under optimum conditions, the proposed sensor exhibits a high sensitivity with a linear range from 0 to 12.0μM and shows the limit of detection as low as 0.15μM in the determination of 4-NP, which is probably benefits from the tailor-made imprinted cavities for binding 4-NP. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully applied for the determination of 4-NP in real water samples and human urine samples with great potentials for monitoring of 4-NP in environmental application.

ce:section.ce:para: 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), one of the deleterious nitrophenol compounds, which is widely used by the medical and chemical industries in daily life, has aroused considerable interest because of its toxic and persistent effects on human health and environment. 4-NP has high toxicity and carcinogenicity even at very low concentration, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has provided the limited concentration of 4-NP in drinking water is 60ngmL−1 (Wei et al., 2011). Thus, exploring a sensitive and precise method of 4-NP detection is of great importance. Various analytical methods have been reported for 4-NP determination, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Thompson et al., 1996; Guo et al., 2004), electrophoresis and electrochemical methods (Huang et al., 2003; Li et al., 2013), fluorescent monitoring (Xiao et al., 2007). Compared with these approaches, fluorescence spectroscopy for 4-NP analysis is superior to others owing to its excellent performance, including simple operation, short manipulation time, fast response, low cost and simple sample pretreatment (Li et al., 2010).

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), have attracted wide attention as a fascinating class of fluorescence-based nanoparticles, which have been spotlighted to be a promising substitute to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) because of their excellent properties, such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, good chemical stability and attractive photoluminescence (PL) properties (Karthik et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013). CDs have potential for a wide range of technologies including bioimaging (Li et al., 2011), solid-state fluorescence (Chen et al., 2016), light-emitting diodes (LEDs) (Li et al., 2016), sensors (Wang et al., 2011; Lei et al., 2015) and others. In terms of applying the eco-friendly CDs in real device applications, CDs should be embedded in a proper solid matrix or be prepared in solid-state architectures. Recently, fluorophore-immobilized inorganic nanomaterials such as SiO2, Al2O3, and TiO2 have been commonly used as chemosensory materials, taking advantages from both photochemistry and nanotechnology (Wang et al., 2010; Gimeno et al., 2008; Kim et al., 2010). Particularly, silica as host matrix for optical chemosensing is preferable mainly lies in its anomalously high stability, especially in aqueous media, optical transparency and chemical inertness. Its high surface-to-volume ratio also provides more sites for specific coupling reactions that permeates to the analytes, remains CDs’ PL properties and avoids the leaching of CDs (Li et al., 2012).

To improve the selectivity of CD-based sensors, a molecular imprinting technique has been introduced to design and construct tailor-made sensors. The obtained molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) exhibit a greater affinity for the template molecules than the other structurally related compounds (Liu et al., 2014). The satisfactory predetermination, stability selectivity and practicability make the MIPs one of the most competitive materials in the field of separation, catalysis and sensors (Orozco et al., 2013; Lian et al., 2012; Wulff et al., 2011). Recently, molecular imprinting has been combined with fluorescent sensor techniques to construct fluorescent sensors for superior performance, on this basis, the recognition and detection of 4-NP have been reported. Shi et al. incorporated MIPs in graphene-quantum-dot-based sensing system for 4-NP detection (Zhou et al., 2014). Yan et al. prepared an imprinted polymer thin film at the surface of ZnO nanorods for selectively recognizing 4-NP (Wei et al., 2015). However, the single-intensity-based sensing is usually compromised by other factors, involved the probe concentration, the drifts of the optoelectronic system, or environmental effects. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors can avoid these problems thank to its built-in correction with improved sensitivity and accuracy. They are simply obtained by incorporated two different fluorophores in one nanoparticle, one fluorophore as reference and another as a response moiety. Given this, it is still a great need for the development of successful ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes for the determination of 4-NP.

Herein, we developed a simple method to prepare fluorescent nanoparticles for ratiometric detection of 4-NP by incorporating CDs with YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles (NPs) within silica via a facile sol-gel method. The Eu3+-activated yttrium orthovanadate (YVO4:Eu3+), a well-known rare-earth doped inorganic luminescent material, has been used as an important red phosphor. Huignard et al. achieved transparent aqueous dispersions of 10nm sized YVO4:Eu3+ colloids based on the aqueous precipitation reaction by the addition of a citrate ligand (Huignard et al., 2002). YVO4:Eu3+ NPs are an efficient photoluminescent material and have a potential to be used as luminescent probes. The 4-NP imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors were constructed by incorporating CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ NPs within silica networks through silylation reaction by one pot synthesis procedure, which can simplify the synthetic process while maintain the sensitivity of ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The selectivity was guaranteed by molecular imprinting technique, the imprinted cavities were surrounded by CDs, whose emission can be suppressed by the addition of 4-NP, while YVO4:Eu3+ emission almost remains constant. It turned out that the quenching efficiency of the hybrid NPs with 4-NP is greatly amplified than that of the corresponding pure CDs solution. The feasibility of the hybrid NPs for 4-NP detection in real water samples and human urine samples were also investigated.

Yttrium nitrate hexahydrate (Y(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.99%), europium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.9%), sodium orthovanadate (Na3VO4, 98%), ammonia aqueous solution (25–28%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99.99% metal basis), sodium citrate and anhydrous citric were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS) was purchased from Shenda Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Petroleum ether, ethanol, methanol and acetic acid were obtained from Tianjin Reagent Plant (Tianjin, China). 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), hydroquinone and phenol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagen Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the initial chemicals in this work were used without further purification and deionized water was used throughout this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the YVO4:Eu3+ NPs colloid
ce:section.ce:para: YVO4:Eu3+ NPs colloid was synthesized according to a reported method (Huignard et al., 2002). The whole synthesis was carried out in water at 60°C. A 0.1molL−1 solution of (Y, Eu)(NO3)3 (10mL and 1mmol) was mixed with a 0.1molL−1 solution of sodium citrate (7.5mL and 0.75mmol). This led to the formation of a white precipitate of lanthanide citrate, which was completely dissolved by the addition of a 0.1molL−1 solution of Na3VO4 (7.5mL and 0.75mmol). The clear and colorless resulting mixture was subsequently aged at 60°C for 3h. An atomic ratio Y/Eu of 9:1 was used at this work.

The synthesis of CDs was similar to the literature (Wang et al., 2011). Briefly, 10mL AEAPMS was placed into a 100mL three-necked flask, and degassed with nitrogen for 15min. Heat was applied, until the temperature reached 240°C, 0.4g anhydrous citric acid was added quickly under vigorous stirring. After 5min, the reaction stopped and cooled to room temperature. Then the product was purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@MIPs, C-Y@MIPs and C-Y@NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: 4-NP molecules are used as the template, and the procedure for C-Y@MIPs (4-NP imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors for short, C represents CDs, Y represents YVO4:Eu3+) preparation is described in Fig. 1(a). C-Y@MIPs were fabricated by incorporating CDs with YVO4:Eu3+ NPs and 4-NP template within silica via a facile sol-gel method. Briefly, to a 50mL flask, 200μL CDs solution, 600μL YVO4:Eu3+ NPs colloid and 50mg 4-NP were added to 30mL ethanol, and then used ultrasound to assist 4-NP dissolution. After stirring for 0.5h, 1mL ammonia aqueous solution and 650μL TEOS were added to the reaction flask, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 12h at room temperature. The resulting product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with ethanol and water to discard the reagents. Original 4-NP template in the imprinted nanoparticles was extracted with a mixture solvent of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, v/v) for several times until no 4-NP could be detected by UV-Vis in the effluent. Considering that the low sensitivity of the UV absorption spectra cannot guarantee the complete elimination of the template. So fluorescence spectrum with higher sensitivity was also employed to confirm the complete removal of template. The fluorescence intensity of as-prepared C-Y@MIPs restored to the level of C-Y@NIPs (nonimprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors for short) can be seen as a symbol of complete removal of the template. The product of C-Y@MIPs was further washed with water, and finally vacuum dried at 60°C for 12h. The C-Y@NIPs were also prepared under the same conditions but without addition of 4-NP. The 4-NP imprinted single fluorescence sensors (CDs@MIPs) were prepared using the above procedure, but without adding YVO4:Eu3+ in the whole procedure.

The FT-IR spectra were taken on a Nicolet Avatar 360 Fourier transformation infrared spectrophotometer. The XRD patterns were recorded using powder X-ray diffraction (MSAL XD-2, Beijing, China) with Cu Kα irradiation and operating at 36kV and 30mA with a scanning step of 0.02° (2θ) and a scanning speed of 8°min−1. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded with a FEI Tecnai 12 transmission electron microscope. The UV-Vis absorption was recorded by an ultraviolet-visible spectrofluorometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). Photoluminescence spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrofluorometer (F-7000, Hitachi). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an AXIS ULTRA DLD, Kratos.

On consideration, water was selected as the dispersion medium. The quenching amount reached the highest when pH was fixed at 7.4. Further experiments were carried out at pH 7.4. The optimal concentration of C-Y@MIPs was fixed at 75mgL−1. A certain amount of C-Y@MIPs was added to a 5.0mL cuvette and a given concentration of analyte standard solution was added sequentially. The fluorescence spectra were collected immediately after fully mixing because the fluorescence spectra became stable soon. The control experiments of C-Y@NIPs and CDs@MIPs were the same as that of C-Y@MIPs.

The proposed procedure was applied to the determination of 4-NP in tap water samples and human urine samples. Tap water samples with different concentrations of 4-NP were added to the sensing system, and the resultant fluorescence spectra were measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Human urine samples were collected from healthy laboratory volunteers, all samples were subjected to a 100-fold dilution before analysis, and no other pretreatments were necessary, and the resultant fluorescence spectra were measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained by repeating the experiment 5 times under the same condition.

Ratiometric fluorescent silica NPs are outstanding due to their improved photostability, adjustable emission, optic transparency, as well as good water dispersivity. The synthesis procedure of ratiometric fluorescence MIPs sensor and the imprinting mechanism are shown in Fig. 1. The 4-NP imprinted ratiometric fluorescence MIPs sensor was prepared by the hybridization of blue fluorescent CDs and red fluorescent YVO4:Eu3+ NPs within silica by covalent bonding. Through the hydrolysis and condensation reactions of TEOS, CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ can be incorporated into silica networks via a one-pot reaction catalyzed by aqueous ammonia solution. In this process, the CDs can interact with 4-NP molecules through the hydrogen bond interactions. These interactions successfully drove the template molecules into the formed silica matrix. After the 4-NP template was extracted from the silica matrix, the MIPs sensor with imprinted cavities was fabricated. Compare to the preparation process for the multi-step core-shell structure, which is time consuming and hard to control, the one pot synthesis is superior. The obtained hybrid nanomaterials show two main emission peaks at 470 and 617nm, which can be assigned to the CDs emission and YVO4:Eu3+ emission. With the addition of 4-NP, the fluorescence of blue CDs embedded in silica can be selectively quenched, whereas YVO4:Eu3+ emission hardly changed, which provides the basis for ratiometric fluorescent determination of 4-NP.

The CDs used herein, were synthesized on the basis of the reported method (Wang et al., 2011). The morphology of CDs was studied by TEM as shown in Fig. 2(a). It can be clearly seen that the CDs display as mono-dispersed, uniform, microspherical particles with an average diameter of ~10nm. The synthesis of CDs involved decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid, accompanying the surface passivation reaction of the amine groups in AEAPMS with the carboxyl groups driven from the pyrolysis species occurred simultaneously. So the obtained CDs carried a large amount of methoxysilane groups, were highly reactive intermediates responsible for bonding with silanol and hydroxyl groups from the hydrolysis and polycondensation of TEOS. Meanwhile, the silanol groups play a crucial role for bonding the metal ions (Y3+, Eu3+) from the YVO4:Eu3+ colloid. The synthesis of colloidal YVO4:Eu3+ using well-known citrate complexing agents to increase the stability of particles and to limit their size. Fig. 2(b) shows spherical crystallites of YVO4:Eu3+ colloid with an average of ~5nm. The citrate ligands presented on the surface of the YVO4:Eu3+ particles make these NPs dispersable in water and cause the formation of transparent solutions. It is directly and feasible to embed CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ in silica without any further modification of their surface groups.

Fig. 2(c) shows the smooth surface and nearly-perfect spherical geometry of C-Y@MIPs. The morphology of C-Y@MIPs microspheres displaying a uniform diameter of ~270nm. HRTEM elemental mappings ( Fig. 3) of C-Y@MIPs are performed to quantify a uniform distribution of Si, C, N, Y, V, O, Eu species and obtain detailed distribution of CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ NPs within C-Y@MIPs spheres. C and N are attributed to CDs, which exist densely in the spheres. While Y, V and Eu are attributed to YVO4:Eu3+, whose contents are less than CDs. The mappings indicates that CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ NPs are evenly distributed throughout the silica framework and confirming that both are highly dispersed in silica spheres. XPS characterization further confirms the existence of CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ on the surface of silica particles (Fig. S1). Signals of bonding energy from the C (1s, 284.9eV), N (1s, 400.2eV), O (1s, 532.5eV), Si (2p, 103.3eV), Y (3d5/2, 153.9eV), and V (2p, 522.6eV) can be seen clearly in panels a-f, respectively. The presence of a relatively strong signal of Si 2p indicates that the microspheres have a dominant SiO2 surface with a small quantity of CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ coated.

FT-IR spectra of CDs, YVO4:Eu3+, C-Y@MIPs are performed in Fig. 4(a). The spectrum of CDs shows characteristic peaks at 1572cm−1 and 3151cm−1 assigning to the vibrations of amide. The characteristic peak at 1640cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of the C˭O groups from the C=ONH units of the CDs. And the vibration signals of Si-O-Si and Si-CH2 at 1190cm−1 and 680cm−1, respectively, also show the formation of AEATMS capped CDs. From the spectrum of YVO4:Eu3+, the strong peak at 810cm−1 and the weak peak at 453cm−1 are respectively ascribed to the vibrational mode of V-O bond and Y-O bond. The peaks at 1398cm−1 and 1581cm−1 are assigned to vibrations of C=OOR in the citrate, which confirm the presence of the citrate ligands. After coating by silica, the intensity of Si-O-Si at 1190cm−1 sharply increased, in the process of TEOS hydrolysis, the silanols can bond the metal ions from YVO4:Eu3+ and methoxysilyl groups from CDs.

Fig. 4(b) shows the XRD patterns of CDs, YVO4:Eu3+, and C-Y@MIPs. YVO4:Eu3+ powder was obtained by drying at 60°C without any high-temperature calcinations and therefore results in poor crystallinity. Moreover, owing to the small size of the particles, the peaks become broaden. Still, the pattern of YVO4:Eu3+ is found to be in agreement with the zircon-type structure of YVO4 (Xie et al., 2010). For CDs, one broad peak is found around 2θ=20°, demonstrates that the CDs are amorphous. Due to the small quantity of YVO4:Eu3+ in C-Y@MIPs and thick silica layer, diffraction peaks of YVO4 are not found, only diffraction pattern of amorphous SiO2 and maybe amorphous CDs exists.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs, YVO4:Eu3+ and C-Y@MIPs are characterized in Fig. 4(c). Blue fluorescent CDs in an aqueous solution exhibit fluorescence maximum at 470nm excited at 360nm while red fluorescence YVO4:Eu3+ show fluorescence maximum at 617nm, which corresponds to 5D0→7F2 electric-dipole transitions under a 275nm excitation. The weak peaks at 593, 653 and 703nm assign to the transitions of 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F3 and 5D0→7F4, respectively. When the blue emissive CDs and red emissive YVO4:Eu3+ NPs were incorporated into silica together, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor shows resolved emission bands under a single wavelength excitation at 330nm, indicating that the hybrid material was successfully prepared.

The dose response of ratiometric fluorescence C-Y@MIPs to 4-NP was examined. On consideration, water was selected as the dispersion medium. As shown in Fig. 4(d), after the addition of 4-NP to C-Y@MIPs in aqueous solution, the C-Y@MIPs sensor had an immediate response, whose fluorescence intensity declined soon and stable for 7 times measurements within 60min. The concentration of C-Y@MIPs from 25 to 150mgL−1 was used to study the quenching effects on the 4-NP detection system in Fig. S1(a), the optimum value was 75mgL−1. CDs emission is sensitive to 4-NP while YVO4:Eu3+ emission as an internal reference. Herein, F0 and F are the PL intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively. F0/F-1 represents the fluorescence quenching efficiency. The pH effect on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of C-Y@MIPs was investigated by changing pH from 4.0 to 11.0. As shown in Fig. S1(b), pH 7.4 was used as working pH in the detection of 4-NP in this work. In order to get effective C-Y@MIP, condition optimization experiments were carried out, such as the amounts of TEOS. As seen in Fig. S2(c), 650μL TEOS was a better dosage in the following tests.

The CDs emission intensity of C-Y@MIPs turned out to decrease sensitively in the presence of 4-NP molecules. The analyte binding will lead to the quenching of CDs fluorescence through two possible pathways: the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and the photoinduced eletron transfer (PET) between CDs and 4-NP molecules. As shown in Fig. S2(d), there is a spectral overlap between the emission of CDs and the absorption of 4-NP, which suggests that fluorescence resonance energy transfer from CDs to 4-NP might occur. When 4-NP molecules rebind to the binding sites of CDs, the excited-state electrons of CDs return to ground state, the ground state of 4-NP thus transit to the excited states, due to the polar-polar resonance of the photoluminescent donor and the nonemissive analyte receptor (Zhou et al., 2014). Besides, the nitrogen heteroatoms and oxygen-containing groups on the CDs surface enable it to attract 4-NP molecules effectively by hydrogen bonds (Qian et al., 2014). When the hydroxyl of 4-NP interact with the oxygen-containing groups of CDs, the long pair electrons of groups is available for photoinduced electron transfer, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence (Zhou et al., 2015).

Fig. 5 shows the effects of 4-NP concentration on the PL intensities of (a) pure CDs solution, (b) CDs@MIPs, (c) C-Y@NIPs, and (d) C-Y@MIPs, respectively. The blue fluorescence at 470nm decreased significantly along with the increasing of the 4-NP concentration in the range of 0-12.0μM, which indicates that the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by 4-NP. To explore the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs with 4-NP, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to evaluate the differences in quenching efficiency for pure CDs solution, C-Y@NIPs, and C-Y@MIPs, respectively. The quenching effect of 4-NP molecules can be described using the following Stern-Volmer equation (Chen and Rosenzweig, 2002):
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
C

where F
0 and F are the fluorescent intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively, C is the concentration of 4-NP, and Ksv is the quenching constant of the quencher. Fig. 6(a) shows the Stern-Volmer plots of pure CDs solution, C-Y@NIP, C-Y@MIP, respectively. A liner Stern-Volmer relationship is observed at the 4-NP concentrations from 0 to 12.0μM. It is clearly to observe, C-Y@MIPs exhibit about 3 times fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) than that of C-Y@NIPs and about 10 times fluorescence quenching efficiency than that of pure CDs solution. Pure CDs solution only exhibits a slight fluorescence quenching with 4-NP analyte. In the case of CDs embedded silica nanoparticles, a single 4-NP molecule is able to interact with all the dense fluorophores located nearby causing their non-radiative deactivation when excited (Montalti et al., 2005). The large surface area of nanoparticles can provide an enriching effect to 4-NP species, maximizing the quenching efficiency of fluorescence (Gao et al., 2008). Compare to C-Y@NIPs, the recognition sites on the C-Y@MIPs have better steric matching ability with the template molecules, so more template molecules have been allowed to adsorb on the molecular imprinted matrix. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by 4-NP, whereas the red fluorescence of YVO4:Eu3+ was little affected by 4-NP. A minor decline of the emission at 617nm of YVO4:Eu3+ might mainly attributes to the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of CDs upon the addition of 4-NP. Inset of Fig. 5(d) illustrates the corresponding plots which show a liner relation between fluorescence intensity ratio (F617/F470) against the 4-NP concentration. A good linearity was obtained in the range of 0-12.0μM. And the detection limit was calculated to be 0.15μM based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3, which is much lower than the maximum concentration (about 0.45μM) of 4-NP in drinking water permitted by US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).

In order to illustrate the selectivity of C-Y@MIPs towards the template molecule 4-NP, control experiments concerning other phenols with similar structure (such as 2-NP, phenol and hydroquinone ) were carried out. As shown in Fig. 6(b), C-Y@MIPs had a large fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F-1) than that of C-Y@NIPs. And the quenching amount in FL intensity of C-Y@MIPs for 4-NP was more obvious than other phenols. Because of their absolutely different absorption spectra from 4-NP (Zhou et al., 2014), resulting little fluorescence resonance energy transfer from CDs to other phenols. Among then, 2-NP has similar optical and chemical properties to 4-NP, but the quenching efficiency to 2-NP is smaller than that of 4-NP. The results implied the imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe could be applied to selective detection of 4-NP.

To investigate the practical application of the proposed method in real samples, the sensing of 4-NP in tap water and human urine were carried out. As no 4-NP was detectable in tap water samples and human urine samples, a recovery study was carried out and the corresponding results were listed in Table S1. Upon the exposure to real tap water spiked with 0–12.0μM 4-NP, the estimated recoveries of the measurements and the RSDs are satisfactory, which also demonstrates that the sensor is to be free of any other influence in tap water and human urine. Therefore, the C-Y@MIPs sensor has the potential applicability for 4-NP detection in real samples. It is worthwhile to compare the performance of the fluorescent sensor based on C-Y@MIPs sensor with other sensors available in literature, as shown in Table S2. It can be seen that the C-Y@MIPs sensor offered acceptable detection limit compared with other sensors. This method is therefore an ideal candidate for detection of 4-NP with built-in correction.

In summary, a simple and effective strategy has been carried out for one pot synthesis of 4-NP imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors that were constructed by incorporating CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ NPs within silica networks through silylation reaction. The proposed method was confirmed to be simple, rapid, and reliable for determining non-fluorescent 4-NP. The obtained ratiometric fluorescence sensors have a linear range of 0-12.0μM and the limit of detection as low as 0.15μM in the determination of 4-NP, which reveals a prospective application in the field of environmental monitoring and evaluation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51203053, 21371062 and 51372091), the Foundation for High-level Talents in Higher Education of Guangdong Province, the Project for Construction of High-level University in Guangdong Province, and the Teamwork Projects funded by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. S2013030012842).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.07.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Chromatogr. B: Biomed. Sci. Appl
